content.full_text:
I

California
Fair Political
Practices Commission

April 12, 1995

Ms. Amy Rueda
District Office of
Assemblywoman Grace F. Napolitano
P.O. Box 408
Norwalk, CA 90650

Re: Your Request for Confirmation
of Telephone Advice
Our File No. I-95-99

Dear Ms. Rueda:

We have received your letter dated March 21, 1995, seeking
written confirmation of the telephone advice I provided to you on
behalf of Assemblywoman Napolitano regarding an event honoring
women of the 58th Assembly District.1/

Based on the facts provided in your telephone inquiry and
reiterated in your letter, the provisions of the Political Reform
Act2/ would not prohibit Assemblywoman Napolitano's participation
at the April 1st luncheon. The Assemblywoman's role included
mailing less than 200 invitations and coordinating the
presentation of the awards. No funds were collected by
Assemblywoman Napolitano as each attendee paid her luncheon cost
directly to the restaurant.

1/     Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the
immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice.
(Government Code Section 83114; 2 Cal. Code of Regs. Section
18329(c)(3).)

2/     Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory
references are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.
Commission regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations
Section 18000, et seq. All references to regulations are to Title
2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.

428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento, CA 95812 • (916) 322-5660

.I- 95-99
page 2

If you have other questions regarding this matter, please
contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

[signature]

Lynda Cassady
Assistant Division Chief
Technical Assistance Division

sections.conclusion: Based on the facts provided in your telephone inquiry and reiterated in your letter, the provisions of the Political Reform Act would not prohibit Assemblywoman Napolitano's participation at the April 1st luncheon. The Assemblywoman's role included mailing less than 200 invitations and coordinating the presentation of the awards. No funds were collected by Assemblywoman Napolitano as each attendee paid her luncheon cost directly to the restaurant.
sections.question_synthetic: Whether Assemblywoman Napolitano's participation in an April 1st luncheon event honoring women of the 58th Assembly District, including mailing invitations and coordinating award presentations, would violate the Political Reform Act.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The FPPC concluded that Assemblywoman Napolitano's participation at the luncheon would not violate the Political Reform Act. The permissible conduct included mailing fewer than 200 invitations, coordinating award presentations, and having attendees pay the restaurant directly rather than collecting funds through the Assemblywoman.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document is confirmation of prior informal telephone advice. No formal QUESTION/CONCLUSION sections with headers, but question and conclusion are clearly identifiable in the letter body. Footnotes indicate this is informal assistance without statutory immunity. File number 1-95-99 matches document ID 95-099.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Whether Assemblywoman Napolitano's participation in an April 1st luncheon event honoring women of the 58th Assembly District, including mailing invitations and coordinating award presentations, would violate the Political Reform Act.

CONCLUSION: Based on the facts provided in your telephone inquiry and reiterated in your letter, the provisions of the Political Reform Act would not prohibit Assemblywoman Napolitano's participation at the April 1st luncheon. The Assemblywoman's role included mailing less than 200 invitations and coordinating the presentation of the awards. No funds were collected by Assemblywoman Napolitano as each attendee paid her luncheon cost directly to the restaurant.

embedding.summary: The FPPC confirmed prior telephone advice that Assemblywoman Napolitano's participation in an April 1st luncheon event honoring women of her district would not violate the Political Reform Act, based on her limited role and the fact that attendees paid the restaurant directly.
